source: Rocky Linux 'About systemd' guide - Unit types

options: A synchronization point that groups other units (often to start enabled services at boot)
questions.why: A target is a grouping/synchronisation unit (see man 5 systemd.target); services, sockets, mounts etc. are other unit types.

questions.q: Which unit type schedules the activation of another unit (like a cron job)?
questions.why: A timer unit schedules activation of another unit; path units activate based on filesystem path state.

questions.q: Which unit type restricts resources via Linux control groups (cgroups)?
questions.why: A slice unit groups processes into a cgroup hierarchy for resource control; scope units manage externally-created processes.

options: Activates a unit based on the state of a filesystem path (e.g. whether it exists)
questions.why: path units provide path-based activation - starting a service when a path appears/changes.

questions.q: Which unit type represents a mount point that is mounted automatically on access/boot?
questions.why: An automount unit mounts on demand; a plain mount unit is an explicitly managed mount point.
